Then cooperation and now we're moving from compliments to cooperation, which what we teach in the certified practitioner program and one study that we show you there is about labor and contract negotiators and to a very complicated algorithm. At the end, they define those people as average or skilled. Complicated measurements, simple defined outcome. And then they looked how these people act and these are professional negotiators. This is what they do for a living. So the average negotiator was talking 90% of the time about what they want and 10% of the time what the other person wants. The skilled negotiator was talking 60% of the time what they want and 40% of the time about what the other person wants. But there's one more thing that even interests me more is the order. The skilled negotiator started to find out what the other person want. The 40% of their investment in the other person came at the beginning. They showed that cooperation to that other person. It's not only about me, it's about us. So they invested not 10, but 40%. So 400% more into the other person. Professional negotiators. And this was the main thing that differentiates the average from the skilled one. You all know this research probably, but what I prepared for you, there's a study done and they analyzed 32 studies involving 5,000 subjects, 5,000 professional negotiators. And that research was investigating how well are we to define collaboration? How well are we to raise that to awareness that we want to collaborate with the other person? Again, it's not done by me or average people, professional negotiators. 50% failed. And I think if I look at the data, it's right. I have a story, and this is a story from a guy that I encountered, and you know him as well, Lyle. What he did, he was a guy that became from wreck to riches. He was not always my favorite person, but I love this story, especially because I think the story will associate why he wasn't always my favorite person. This man had an ego. Another small one, a quite big one. Let's say his name is David. And David had a horrible childhood. His parents were alcoholics, they were abusive. He was at 11 different schools in nine years, and he fought himself to become an entrepreneur, which gained him my respect, and he was successful. By doing that, that came with building a certain ego, which you might understand, coming from nothing and suddenly being important, does something to people. What it did to him, he was a financial planner, and he had an advisory firm in London. And there were a lot of Indian people, and no offense. In the morning, I had two people from India, so there's nothing what I want to say offensive. It was David's problem. His problem was that he thought when Indian people come in, they always want a bigger mortgage than they can afford. And it's a waste of my time. That was his mindset. And at one day, for no reason, what he did. Another Indian came, Mr. Shah, walked into his office. And suddenly he had an epiphany. He said, David, stop being you. Stop being this ego-driven man who thinks, who knows it all. Be a good guy, help people. That's what he did. He moved away all his ego, and I can promise you, he had a lot. So he moved it. And Mr. Shah came in, and he started talking, and he did everything to help Mr. Shah. And what was eventually the outcome? Mr. Shah was well known in the London area. Mr. Shah had multiple companies. He could easily get the mortgage he needed. But the Indian system, I don't know the exact words in English, they have like this ranking system. You know, the word, honey? Past system. Car system, yes. But Mr. Shah turned out to be superstar black belt at the top of the pyramid. So not only Mr. Shah got what he wanted, he really liked David, because David found a way to like him. What was the consequence? Mr. Shah sent so many clients to David. He made so much revenue out of that conversation. Why? A simple thing. Move your ego away. And I strongly believe in that. Whatever, which position you are, wherever you are in the world, you're never better than another human being. I actually believe when you have it better than another human being, you should step up. And definitely move your ego away. Albert person, it's not a pre-programmed thing. My advice to you is become more interested than interesting. In other words, listen more, talk less. When you listen more, I promise you, what I found out during my life, that that other person, no matter how you started with the relation, there is something you can like about it. And when you find that, the consequences, the results, and the impact are amazing. Thank you. That's awesome. Thank you so much, boss. Last question of the day. I don't see any hands risen. So what I like to do, I like to thank you and I want to leave you with an advice. For me, when I was a young boy, just starting my career, I didn't like school. I could learn easily, but I was too late. I didn't want to finish university. At one moment I was asked by a friend of mine, boss, don't you want to be start to work? I was 21 and I was support to financial advisors. But within a month I saw how much commission they made. So my eyes were open and I suddenly liked learning because I thought, wow, now I can apply it. So quickly I had more degrees than the average financial advisor. So I said, I want to be an advisor. And the boss said, no, you're way too young. Nobody trusts you. And then luckily for me, I had some self-esteem or self-confidence. I said, then I go find a company that let me do it. I found it and I was the most successful, not to pitch myself. We all know that's wrong, but I was the most successful financial advisor they ever had. So I was wondering, how on earth is that possible? Why I'm just a simple guy from a simple family and how can I be more successful than all these people with all these years of experience? That was what interested me. Later on, companies hired me to teach other advisors to do what I did. You know what I found out? The only thing that I did different was making time to get a connection with my clients. It was the only thing, but I didn't, I almost didn't have anything else in the beginning. I was an easy talker, so I just talked about their life. But I kept doing it. I loved it. First of all, and the more I knew about financial and laws and tax laws, I kept on doing it. I had a 96% closing rate. Why? Simple. I was interested in them. So that's my last tip. That's a fantastic tip. And we just all have to keep that to the front of our minds and always think, what about this person can I find that I like and focus on that? Thank you, Arli. Thank you all for joining. I hope it was valuable and I look forward to seeing you next time. Arli, last words for you. Take care, everybody. If you have any questions as always, email us at support at cheldini.com. Robert, I see your link. I'll check that out and shoot you an email. Talk to you guys later. Fantastic. Have a great day, everyone. Thank you. Thank you. Bye-bye.
